title: Sophia AI Assessment — Numa (Fixed Ops)
summary: This document provides an assessment of Numa AI for Fixed Ops, identifying its potential to improve customer satisfaction and service booking show rates, but also highlighting significant risks related to trust, control, and advisor workflow. It includes a scorecard, lists missing vendor evidence, red flags, and critical questions for the vendor to address before proceeding.
text:
Sophia AI Assessment — Numa (Fixed Ops)
Mode: Light
Decision Authority: Dealer Principal (Final)
Ramp Window: 180 days
Win Definition (12 months): Increase customer satisfaction and service booking show rate.
Narrative recommendation
Numa is aimed at the right Fixed Ops pain: missed calls, appointment scheduling, and status updates—
areas that directly impact customer satisfaction and booked work. If it reliably captures after-hours demand
and reduces “where’s my car?” traffic, it can support your win metrics.
Your primary risks are  trust and control, not features. You’ve flagged it as a  black box, and advisor
workflow changes may concentrate negative interactions (advisors become the last-stop escalation), raising
burnout risk and potentially harming the very satisfaction you’re trying to improve. Proceed only with
guardrails that force transparency, tight escalation controls, and explicit data-use limits.
Scorecard (0–100)
Category Weight Score (0–10)Weighted Pts
AI Authenticity 20 4 8.0
Operational Fit 20 7 14.0
Resource Alignment 15 6 9.0
Transparency / Data 15 3 4.5
ROI Clarity 15 5 7.5
Risk / Exit 10 4 4.0
Change Management 5 4 2.0
Total 100 49.0
Verdict Band:<60 = High Risk / Hype Detected (unless guarded pilot terms are negotiated)
Vendor proof checklist (missing evidence)
Data use & training (in writing): Contract addendum clarifying if/when dealership communications
can be used to train/improve models; ability to opt out.
• 
1
Explainability/QA: Clear audit trail for why the agent responded/escalated (beyond transcripts), plus
QA tooling.
Xtime integration depth: Exact supported actions (create/modify/cancel, capacity rules, advisor
routing, loaners, RO linkage, etc.).
Escalation + CX controls: Hard rules for handoff to humans (repeat caller , upset sentiment, safety,
warranty, price disputes, comebacks).
SLA + uptime + failover: What happens when Numa, phones, or Xtime integration fails.
Compliance responsibility clarity: Written responsibility matrix (recording/consent/TCPA-style
messaging, disclosures, retention).
Red flags
Black box behavior: Hard to predict edge-case handling; edge cases drive CSI loss.
Data rights ambiguity: “Customers are yours” but vendor may use data for improvement/training
with consent.
Advisor experience risk: Advisors may become the complaint desk; burnout + reduced warmth
when a human finally engages.
5 next questions for the vendor
Training/data: Will you contractually commit to no model training on our dealership’s customer
communications (voice/text/chat), including derived insights—yes or no?
Xtime specifics: List the exact Xtime actions supported and what is not supported.
Escalation policy: Show the default escalation tree for upset customers and status calls—what
triggers takeover and how fast?
CX outcomes: Provide comparable-rooftop results with baselines for CSI/NPS, show rate, complaint
rate over 90–180 days.
Exit & portability: On cancellation, can we export all transcripts/recordings/tags/performance
history, and what’s retained after termination?
